notes: Human-reviewed recipe source audit. Use this to separate culinary/source verification from static route eligibility.

kedjenou-ci.official_source_status: No direct Côte d'Ivoire ministry or tourism recipe page was found in web search on 2026-04-27. Treat the named cookbook-derived, encyclopedia, and culinary references below as the current evidence set until an official source is located.
kedjenou-ci.review_summary: The AfroKitchen Kedjenou entry matches the core cross-source pattern: Ivorian/Baoulé or Akan dish, chicken or guinea fowl, sealed low-heat cooking in a canari or tightly sealed pot, little or no added liquid, tomatoes/onions/chile/aromatics, and service with attiéké or rice.
recommended_changes: Keep the no-added-water positioning. Multiple references describe little or no added liquid, while a few modern adaptations use a small amount of water or stock.
Add salt and pepper to the ingredient list or visibly mark them as pantry seasoning because the method already tells cooks to season with salt and pepper.
Do not make bell pepper or green onion required. They appear in some modern recipes, but the current tomato, onion, eggplant, garlic, ginger, chile, and bay profile is defensible.
Keep the guinea fowl substitution. Multiple references describe chicken or guinea hen/fowl as traditional.

sources.title: 196 flavors - Kedjenou
supports: tomato, onion, garlic, eggplant, ginger, bay, chile profile

sources.title: Kathryn Toure - Kedjenou de poulet PDF, adapted from Cuisine de Côte d'Ivoire et d'Afrique de l'Ouest
supports: sealed cooking so vapor does not escape
chicken pieces with tomatoes, garlic, onion, chile or ginger, bay

sources.title: International Cuisine - Côte d'Ivoire Kedjenou
supports: sealed canari or tight Dutch oven method
eggplant, tomato, onion, chile, ginger, bay, garlic profile

sources.title: Marga's International Recipes - Ivorian Stewed Chicken
supports: chicken with onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, bay

sources.title: Kédjénou - French Wikipedia
supports: chicken, bush meat, or rabbit variations
tomato, onion, chile, ginger, aromatic leaves

chakhchoukha-dz.official_source_status: No Algerian ministry or tourism recipe page was found in the first web pass. Current evidence combines culinary references, an encyclopedia entry, and cookbook/community sources.
chakhchoukha-dz.review_summary: The AfroKitchen Chakhchoukha entry matches the core pattern: Algerian torn rougag or thin flatbread served with a tomato-based meat stew, chickpeas, onions, ras el hanout, chile heat, and a communal platter. The main practical gap was that the method used oil, stew seasoning, and water that were not visible in the ingredient list.
recommended_changes: Keep the rougag plus lamb stew structure.
Add cooking oil, stew salt, black pepper, and water as practical ingredients because the method already relies on them.
Consider a later regional variation note for cumin, caraway, garlic, and vegetables because sources vary by region.

sources.title: TasteAtlas - Chakhchoukha authentic recipe
supports: lamb, rougag, tomato, chickpeas, onion, ras el hanout, black pepper, and salt profile

sources.title: The Odehlicious - Algerian Chakhchoukha Recipe
supports: torn rougag in tomato broth with meat or chicken, chickpeas, onion, garlic, tomato paste, and ras el hanout

sources.title: Chakhchoukha - Wikipedia
supports: Algerian dish of rougag pieces with marqa, meat, chickpeas, vegetables, dried chilli, ras el hanout, black pepper, cumin, and salt

sources.title: The Recipes of Mamounette PDF
supports: Constantinian chakhchoukha with crumbled flatbread, red sauce, meat, chickpeas, ras el hanout, and tomato paste

couscous-royal-dz.official_source_status: UNESCO confirms couscous knowledge and consumption practices as shared intangible heritage of Algeria, Mauritania, Morocco, and Tunisia. Recipe-level official guidance for this specific royal version was not found in the first web pass.
couscous-royal-dz.review_summary: The recipe is directionally sound as an Algerian celebratory couscous page, but 'royal' is a flexible restaurant/diaspora style rather than a single fixed official formula. The page needed visible ingredients for onion, oil, water, salt, pepper, ras el hanout, and harissa because the cooking steps already use them.
recommended_changes: Keep the couscoussier steaming method and lamb, merguez, chickpea, turnip, carrot, courgette structure.
Add the broth aromatics and pantry ingredients that already appear in the method.
Use softer copy for the royal-style framing because sources treat couscous as a broad Maghrebi heritage with many local versions.

recipe.description: A celebratory Algerian-style couscous with fluffy steamed semolina, lamb, merguez sausage, chickpeas, turnips, carrots, courgettes, and a fragrant broth.
recipe.story: Couscous is inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list as shared heritage of Algeria, Mauritania, Morocco, and Tunisia. In Algeria, couscous is a family anchor, especially around Friday lunches and celebrations. This royal-style version brings together meat, merguez, vegetables, chickpeas, and broth for a generous feast rather than a single fixed national formula.

sources.title: UNESCO - Couscous intangible heritage listing
supports: couscous production and consumption practices shared by Algeria, Mauritania, Morocco, and Tunisia

sources.title: UNESCO article on couscous traditions
supports: 2020 inscription and shared North African cultural context

sources.title: Algerian couscous - Wikipedia
supports: lamb or beef with chickpeas, onions, carrots, squash, spices, and Algerian variation context

sources.title: Food.com - Algerian Stew for Couscous
supports: lamb, chickpeas, vegetables, and ras el hanout stew served with couscous

makroud-dz.official_source_status: No Algerian ministry or tourism recipe page was found in the first web pass. Current evidence combines recipe references and broader Maghrebi culinary summaries.
makroud-dz.review_summary: The Makroud entry matches the main pattern: diamond semolina pastry, date filling with cinnamon and orange blossom water, frying, then honey coating. The practical gap was that the method used salt and warm water, while the ingredient list did not show them.
recommended_changes: Keep the date, cinnamon, orange blossom water, fried semolina, and honey structure.
Add salt and warm water to the dough ingredients because the method already requires them.
Rename the semolina to coarse or medium semolina in the static page because many references use coarse semolina or a coarse/fine blend for texture.

recipe.story: Makroud is loved across the Maghreb and is part of Algerian patisserie for Eid, weddings, and guest tables. Algerian versions commonly wrap spiced date paste in semolina dough, shape it into diamonds, fry or bake it, then finish it with warm honey or syrup scented with orange blossom water.

append_ingredients.name: warm water
append_ingredients.prep_note: as needed for a pliable dough

sources.title: Fork & Salt - Makroud recipe
supports: semolina dough with warm water and salt, date filling, orange blossom water, frying, and honey syrup

sources.title: Gourmandize - Makrout with Dates and Honey
supports: semolina pastry with date filling, orange flower water, cinnamon, and honey

sources.title: Makroudh - Wikipedia
supports: Maghrebi semolina cookie with date or fig filling and honey association

sources.title: Fatma TV - Date and almond makrout
supports: date paste, cinnamon, orange blossom water, semolina dough, and honey coating

amiwo-bj.official_source_status: A recipe contributed by the EU Delegation to Benin was found in the Africa-Europe Cook Book. No Benin ministry recipe page was found in the first web pass.
amiwo-bj.review_summary: The Amiwo entry matches the core Beninese pattern: cornmeal or corn flour cooked into red tomato-based paste, served with chicken or fish and sauce. The page needed garlic, water, salt, pepper, and a palm-oil/vegetable-oil wording because these appear across sources and in the cooking method.
recommended_changes: Keep cornmeal, tomato, onion, oil, and grilled or fried protein pairing.
Add water, garlic, salt, and black pepper so the ingredient list matches the cooking method and common source patterns.
Allow vegetable oil as a practical fallback, but mention palm oil because several sources treat it as the traditional flavor base.

recipe.story: Amiwo, also called red paste, is a Beninese cornmeal dish cooked with tomato, onion, oil, and seasoning until it sets into a savory mound. It is commonly served for family meals and celebrations with chicken, fish, or a tomato-pepper sauce.

fields.instruction: Heat oil, fry onion and garlic until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add blended tomatoes, salt, and black pepper, then cook until reduced and thick, about 15 minutes.

sources.title: Africa-Europe Cook Book - Amiwo au Poulet
supports: Benin recipe contributed by EU Delegation to Benin, corn flour, tomatoes, oil, onion, garlic, salt, pepper, chili, chicken stock, and special-occasion context

sources.title: A Tour of African Gastronomy - Amiwo with Grilled Chicken
supports: Benin amiwo with corn flour, tomato sauce, oil, onions, tomatoes, broth, salt, pepper, and household ritual context

sources.title: Cuisine228 - Djinkoume Amiwo with chicken
supports: Amiwo in Benin with corn flour, oil, salt, stock or cube, tomato, onion, pepper sauce, and fried chicken

sources.title: Amiwo - Wikipedia
supports: Benin savory cornflour pudding with onions, garlic, black pepper, salt, tomato paste, oil or palm oil, and grilled or fried chicken or fish

wagasi-grille-bj.official_source_status: No current official Benin tourism recipe page was found in the first web pass. Older Benin tourism references are cited indirectly by encyclopedic sources.
wagasi-grille-bj.review_summary: The Wagasi page is directionally sound: wagasi is a West African cow-milk cheese strongly associated with northern Benin and Parakou, commonly fried, boiled, or cooked in sauce, and often eaten with pepper. The practical gap was the piment sauce using lime and salt without listing them.
recommended_changes: Keep wagasi with oil and pepper sauce.
Add lime and salt because the piment sauce method uses them.
Keep paneer or halloumi as substitutions only for readers outside West Africa, not as cultural equivalents.

sources.title: Wagasi - Wikipedia
supports: cow-milk cheese common in northern Benin and Parakou, also sold fried with pepper for dipping

sources.title: ComeChop Africa - Wagashi Beninese Fried Cheese
supports: Beninese fried wagashi and spicy serving context

sources.title: Traditional dairy products in the Republic of Benin
supports: wagashi as a traditional dairy product in Benin

kuli-kuli-bj.official_source_status: No Benin official recipe page was found in the first web pass. Evidence comes from regional summaries and recipe references for a snack eaten across West Africa, including Benin.
kuli-kuli-bj.review_summary: The Kuli-Kuli entry matches the common West African method: roasted peanuts are ground into paste, excess oil is extracted, the paste is seasoned, shaped, and fried until crisp. No static recipe changes were needed in this pass.
recommended_changes: Keep the oil-extraction step as essential.
Keep shapes flexible, since sticks, rings, rounds, and balls all appear in source patterns.
Keep Benin framing as part of a broader West African snack tradition rather than exclusive origin language.

sources.title: Kuli-kuli - Wikipedia
supports: peanut snack popular in Nigeria, Benin, northern Cameroon, and Ghana, made by extracting oil from peanut paste and frying shaped paste

sources.title: Jotscroll - How to Make Kuli Kuli at Home
supports: roast peanuts, grind to paste, extract peanut oil, shape, and fry

sources.title: Shop Africa USA - How to make Kulikuli
supports: spicy crunchy roasted peanut snack eaten across Nigeria, Cameroon, Ghana, and Benin, with oil extraction before frying

morogo-bw.official_source_status: No Botswana government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Botswana travel/culture references, Slow Food Ark of Taste, and local recipe writing.
morogo-bw.review_summary: The AfroKitchen Morogo page is directionally sound: morogo is a Botswana and Southern African leafy-green dish, often using wild spinach, cowpea leaves, amaranth, or pumpkin leaves, cooked with onion, tomato, oil, salt, and sometimes peanuts or peanut butter. The current peanut powder version is defensible as a richer morogo wa dinawa-style variation.
recommended_changes: Keep leafy greens with tomato, onion, oil, salt, and peanut powder.
Keep the serving suggestion with pap, bogobe, or seswaa.
In a later variation note, distinguish plain morogo from morogo wa dinawa/cowpea-leaf versions.

sources.title: Botswana.co.za - The Food of Botswana
supports: morogo as leafy green commonly served with seswaa and pap

sources.title: Goway - Botswana Food and Drink
supports: morogo as assorted leafy greens such as wild spinach or pumpkin leaves, cooked with onions, tomatoes, and spices

sources.title: Slow Food Foundation - Morogo wa Dinawa
supports: cowpea leaves in Botswana, dried and rehydrated, eaten as leaf stew or stirred into porridge

sources.title: Botswana Youth - Morogo wa Dinawa recipe
supports: cowpea leaves with onion, oil, tomato, salt, tomato paste, peanut butter, and spice blend

seswaa-bw.official_source_status: No Botswana government recipe page was found in this pass. Botswana travel/culture references and recipe sources consistently identify seswaa as the national dish.
seswaa-bw.review_summary: The Seswaa page matches the cross-source pattern: beef or goat boiled until tender, shredded or pounded, seasoned very simply with salt and sometimes onion, pepper, or bay in modern home adaptations, then served with pap/bogobe and morogo.
recommended_changes: Keep beef, water, salt, long simmering, pounding/shredding, and bogobe or pap service.
Soften the copy so it does not imply that every version uses only meat, water, and salt while the recipe itself includes onion and optional pepper.
Keep bay leaves out of the core recipe for now because they appear in adapted versions rather than the most stripped-down traditional framing.

recipe.description: Botswana's national dish: beef slow-boiled until falling apart, then shredded and pounded to a tender pulled-meat texture with simple salt-forward seasoning.
recipe.story: Seswaa is the pride of Botswana, served at weddings, funerals, national celebrations, and important family gatherings. The traditional heart of the dish is restrained: meat, water, salt, and patient cooking, with some home versions adding onion or pepper. The simplicity lets Botswana's beef or goat shine.

sources.title: Botswana.co.za - The Food of Botswana
supports: boiled with onion and pepper, then shredded and pounded with salt

sources.title: G Adventures - Seswaa Recipe
supports: bone-in beef, salt, black pepper, bay leaves, water, slow cooking, pounding, and pap plus morogo service

sources.title: Seswaa - Wikipedia
supports: traditional meat dish of Botswana made with beef or goat, often for ceremonies and national events

vetkoek-bw.official_source_status: No Botswana government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence comes from Botswana travel/culture references and Southern African recipe references.
vetkoek-bw.review_summary: The Vetkoek with Mince page matches the Southern African pattern: yeast dough fried into golden rounds, split and filled with savory mince. The main fix was practical: the mince step used salt, pepper, and curry powder, but those seasonings were not visible in the ingredient list.
recommended_changes: Keep magwinya/vetkoek as a Botswana and broader Southern African street-food framing.
Add curry powder, salt, and black pepper to the mince filling ingredients.
Keep sweet fillings as a note, not the default, because this route is specifically the mince version.

sources.title: Botswana.co.za - The Food of Botswana
supports: vetkoek as deep-fried dough cut in half and filled with curried mince

sources.title: FinGlobal - Magwinya recipe
supports: magwinya as sweet fried dough balls often filled with curried mince, chicken mayo, butter, or jam

sources.title: Vetkoek - Wikipedia
supports: yeast dough fried bread, often served with curried mince, common in South Africa, Namibia, and Botswana

babenda-bf.official_source_status: FAO indexes Babenda from a Burkina Faso local-products cookbook, and a Burkina recipe PDF gives a detailed ingredient and preparation pattern. No tourism-ministry recipe page was found in this pass.
babenda-bf.review_summary: The prior Babenda page had the right broad idea of greens and soumbala, but the grain base was off. Stronger Burkina sources describe modern Babenda as sorrel and African spinach or amaranth leaves cooked with rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, dried fish, pepper, salt, and shea butter or oil for serving.
recommended_changes: Replace millet-flour porridge framing with rice, leafy greens, and pounded peanuts.
Make soumbala a small seasoning rather than the main body of the dish.
Add dried fish, pepper, salt, and water so the method matches the source pattern.

recipe.description: A nourishing Burkina Faso pot of leafy greens, rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, and dried fish, finished with shea butter, pepper, and salt.
recipe.story: Babenda is a Mossi-rooted Burkinabe dish born from resilience during difficult harvest years, when families stretched edible leaves with a little grain and water. Modern versions are richer, often using sorrel leaves, African spinach or amaranth, rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, and dried fish, then shared hot with shea butter or oil.

fields.name: leafy greens
fields.prep_note: sorrel leaves and African spinach, amaranth, or spinach, chopped

fields.title: Cook the greens
fields.instruction: Bring a pot of water to a boil. Add the chopped African spinach or amaranth first, then add sorrel leaves once the greens begin to soften. Simmer for about 15 minutes.
fields.tip: Sorrel brings tang, while amaranth or spinach gives the dish body.

fields.title: Add rice and peanuts
fields.instruction: Add the rinsed rice and pounded peanuts to the greens. Stir well so the peanuts disperse through the pot.

fields.title: Season the pot
fields.instruction: Add soumbala and dried fish powder, then stir carefully with a wooden spoon so the mixture does not stick.

fields.title: Simmer slowly
fields.instruction: Cover and cook over low heat for 25 to 30 minutes, stirring often, until the rice is tender and the greens and peanuts form a thick, hearty mixture.
Serve hot with shea butter or oil, pepper, and salt at the table. Babenda can also sit beside toh for a fuller meal.

sources.title: FAO INPhO - Babenda source listing
supports: Babenda sourced from Recettes a base de produits locaux du Burkina Faso

sources.title: Recette du Babenda mets du Burkina PDF
supports: sorrel leaves, African spinach, rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, dried fish, pepper, salt
serve hot with shea butter or oil

sources.title: Creditinfo UEMOA Cookbook 2024
supports: Babenda as Mossi-origin dish now loved widely in Burkina Faso

poulet-bicyclette-bf.official_source_status: No Burkina Faso ministry recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Burkina media/culture references and culinary recipe sources.
poulet-bicyclette-bf.review_summary: The Poulet Bicyclette page is directionally sound: local free-range chicken grilled over charcoal with a sharp marinade of garlic, mustard or vinaigrette, citrus or vinegar, oil, chile, herbs, salt, and pepper. The main practical gap was missing salt, pepper, and herbs in the ingredient list, plus copy that over-explained the bicycle name.
recommended_changes: Keep free-range chicken and charcoal-grilled maquis/street-food framing.
Add salt, black pepper, and parsley to match common marinade patterns.
Soften the etymology: the name is tied to local chickens and market/street context, not one single origin story.

recipe.description: Grilled local free-range chicken marinated in mustard, citrus, garlic, chile, oil, and herbs, then cooked over charcoal until smoky and crisp.
recipe.story: Poulet bicyclette is the beloved local chicken of Burkina Faso's maquis and street grills. The name is tied to small, firm, free-range birds and the market life around them, including vendors transporting chickens by bicycle or motorbike. The dish is simple, smoky, and best eaten hot with onions, fries, rice, attieke, or a sharp pepper sauce.

fields.instruction: Mix mustard, lime juice, garlic, parsley, cayenne, oil, salt, and pepper into a paste.

sources.title: L'Economiste du Faso - Poulet bicyclette un espoir a Bousse
supports: poulet bicyclette as a Burkina local chicken value chain and common street food context

sources.title: Poulet-bicyclette - French Wikipedia
supports: traditional free-range West African chicken, bicycle-market naming, Burkina Faso local names, open-air raising, firm flavorful meat, wood-fire cooking

sources.title: A Culinary Journey - Poulet Bicyclette
supports: local free-range chicken south of the Sahara and Burkina bicycle-market naming context

riz-gras-bf.official_source_status: No Burkina Faso ministry recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines culinary references, food-history writing, and encyclopedic summaries.
riz-gras-bf.review_summary: The Riz Gras page is directionally sound as a Burkina-style one-pot tomato rice with meat, oil, onion, tomato paste, vegetables, and rice. The practical gaps were garlic, water or stock, salt, pepper, and chile, all common in source patterns or used by the method.
recommended_changes: Keep rice cooked directly in the seasoned meat-tomato broth.
Add garlic, cooking water or stock, salt, pepper, and optional hot pepper.
Avoid calling every spice mandatory because regional and household versions vary widely.

fields.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Season meat with salt and black pepper, then brown on all sides. Remove and reserve.

Fry onions and garlic in the same pot until golden. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add 5 cups water or stock, optional hot pepper, and the browned meat. Bring to a boil and simmer until meat is tender, about 25 minutes.

sources.title: Kosher World Kitchen - Riz Gras
supports: Burkina Faso rice dish with chicken, oil, onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, carrots, bell pepper, salt, bouillon, stock, and optional habanero

sources.title: Polkadot Passport - Riz Gras Burkina Faso
supports: riz gras as Burkina national dish, one-pot rice with tomato, onion, vegetables, oil, rice, and stock

sources.title: Riz gras - Wikipedia
supports: rice-based dish in Burkinabe and other West African cuisines, often served at parties in urban Burkina Faso

burundian-isombe.official_source_status: No Burundi government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Burundi travel/tour references, a children/culture PDF, and recipe references.
burundian-isombe.review_summary: The Isombe page matches the core pattern of cassava or manioc leaves simmered with aromatics and palm oil, often served with ubugari. The main gap was that many Burundi/Rwanda/Great Lakes recipes include peanut butter or pounded peanuts, and the page did not make that component visible.
recommended_changes: Keep cassava leaves, palm oil, onion, garlic, tomato, eggplant, water, salt, and optional dried fish.
Add peanut butter or pounded peanuts and optional bell pepper/spinach variation.
Keep fish optional because vegetarian and fish/meat versions both appear.

fields.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Stir in peanut butter or pounded peanuts. Cover and simmer, stirring occasionally, until leaves are very tender and the sauce is thick.
fields.tip: Burundian cooks simmer isombe patiently so the cassava leaves soften and the peanut or palm-oil richness rounds out the sauce.

sources.title: M Travel and Tours Burundi - Isombe
supports: Isombe as loved Burundian cassava-leaf stew, palm oil in some versions, paired with meat, fish, or vegetarian meals

sources.title: One Globe Kids - Isombe and Ubugari Burundi PDF
supports: manioc leaves, spring onions, eggplant, spinach, green peppers, palm oil, peanut butter, and ubugari pairing

sources.title: FoodieAtlas - Isombe Burundi
supports: cassava leaves with onion, garlic, tomato, oil, salt, broth or water, and optional protein

mukeke.official_source_status: No Burundi government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines A Tour of African Gastronomy and Burundi travel/tour recipe references.
mukeke.review_summary: The Mukeke page correctly identifies the dish as a prized Lake Tanganyika fish. The page should name mukeke first and tilapia only as a substitute, and it should include parsley/herb notes used in sauce or marinade references. Grilled and pan-sauced versions both appear in sources.
recommended_changes: Rename the fish ingredient to mukeke or whole tilapia so the recipe does not look like generic tilapia first.
Add parsley as an optional herb and include it in the marinade instruction.
Fill the empty serving pairing with ubugari, fried plantains, tomato-onion salad, or rice.

recipe.best_served_with: Ubugari, fried plantains, tomato-onion salad, or rice

fields.instruction: Score fish deeply on both sides. Mix lemon juice, garlic, ginger, parsley, oil, salt, pepper, and paprika. Rub marinade all over and inside the fish.

sources.title: A Tour of African Gastronomy - Mukeke
supports: mukeke from Lake Tanganyika, widely consumed in Bujumbura, with cow butter, onion, garlic, parsley, pepper, water, and salt

sources.title: Burundi Travel - How to prepare mukeke in Burundi
supports: mukeke as emblematic Lake Tanganyika fish, with tomato, onion, garlic, lemon, salt, oil, water, optional red pepper and bay

sources.title: Sunheron - What to Eat in Burundi
supports: mukeke and other core Burundian dishes such as isombe and ubugali

ubugari.official_source_status: No Burundi government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Burundi travel/tour writing and a culture/education recipe PDF.
ubugari.review_summary: The Ubugari page is sound as a cassava-flour staple served with beans, isombe, fish, or meat sauces. The main improvement is to fill the empty serving field and make the optional oil or butter used in some recipes visible.
recommended_changes: Keep cassava flour, boiling water, vigorous stirring, and dome shaping.
Add optional oil or butter and a clear serving pairing.
Keep beans as a companion, not as the only definition of ubugari.

recipe.best_served_with: Isombe, beans, grilled fish, or meat sauce

fields.instruction: Bring water to a rolling boil in a heavy pot. Add salt and optional oil or butter.

sources.title: Burundi Travel - Ubugari at the heart of Burundian cuisine
supports: ubugari made from cassava or maize flour, water brought to boil, flour gradually incorporated, served with beans, isombe, meat, and sauced dishes

sources.title: One Globe Kids - Isombe and Ubugari Burundi PDF
supports: manioc meal, water, salt, oil or butter, thick texture, dome shaping, eaten with isombe

sources.title: Fork and Salt - Ubugari Recipe
supports: Burundian cassava fufu staple and sauce pairings

eru-cm.official_source_status: No Cameroon ministry recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence includes encyclopedic and recipe references that agree on the core ingredient pattern.
eru-cm.review_summary: The Eru page is sound: finely shredded eru or okok leaves cooked with waterleaf or spinach, palm oil, crayfish, smoked fish, cow skin or beef, and served with garri, water fufu, or fufu corn. No static recipe changes were needed.
recommended_changes: Keep eru leaves, waterleaf, palm oil, smoked fish, cow skin, crayfish, salt, and dry/oily texture.
Keep garri and fufu corn as serving pairings.
Consider a later note for beef as another common protein option.

sources.title: Eru (soup) - Wikipedia
supports: Cameroon Bayangi/South-West specialty, eru or okok leaves, waterleaf or spinach, palm oil, crayfish, smoked fish, cow skin or beef, garri or water-fufu

sources.title: StoresGo - Cameroonian Eru
supports: eru leaves, waterleaf, cow skin, palm oil preparation pattern

koki-beans-cm.official_source_status: No Cameroon ministry recipe page was found in this pass, but the national intangible-heritage context is referenced in secondary sources. Evidence agrees on black-eyed peas, palm oil, chile, leaf wrapping, and steaming.
koki-beans-cm.review_summary: The Koki Beans page is sound on technique and ingredients. The only change is copy: sources place koki across Littoral, South-West, and West Cameroon rather than only a single Bamileke grasslands frame.
recommended_changes: Keep black-eyed peas, palm oil, chile, banana leaves, water, and steaming.
Soften regional copy to include broader Cameroonian origins and associations.
Keep plantains as a serving pairing.

recipe.story: Koki is a golden Cameroonian bean cake associated with communities across the Littoral, South-West, and West regions. Black-eyed peas or cowpeas are soaked, peeled, crushed into a batter, enriched with red palm oil, wrapped in banana leaves, and steamed until set. The result is dense, fragrant, and deeply comforting.

sources.title: Koki (food) - Wikipedia
supports: Cameroonian black-eyed pea cake, palm oil, chilli pepper, banana leaves, water, steamed paste, regional origin across Littoral, South-West, and West

sources.title: Wikimedia Commons - Koki Beans
supports: Cameroonian specialty made from black-eyed peas and palm oil, often cooked in banana leaves

ndole-cm.official_source_status: No Cameroon ministry recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence includes WIPO gastronomic-tourism material, encyclopedic summaries, and multiple culinary references.
ndole-cm.review_summary: The Ndole page is sound as a Cameroonian bitter-leaf and peanut stew. The main practical gap was missing water or broth, salt, and white/black pepper, while the method already depends on seasoning and peanut blending liquid.
recommended_changes: Keep bitter leaves, peanuts, crayfish, garlic, onion, palm oil, and prawns as a seafood-focused version.
Add water or broth, salt, and pepper to make the method actionable.
Later, add a variation note for beef, smoked fish, or shrimp combinations.

fields.instruction: Roast raw peanuts in a dry pan until golden. Grind in a blender with a little water or broth to form a smooth paste.
Add the prawns to the stew and cook for 8-10 minutes until pink and cooked through. Taste and adjust with salt and white pepper.

sources.title: Ndole - Wikipedia
supports: Cameroonian national dish of stewed ground peanuts, crayfish, garlic, onions, and ndoleh leaves, originating in Douala

sources.title: Nkosi Agro - Cameroonian Ndole
supports: bitterleaf, beef or pork, dried shrimp, peanut paste, onion, garlic, ginger, palm oil, Maggi, salt, white pepper, water

sources.title: WIPO scoping study Cameroon
supports: ndole as a Cameroon gastronomic-tourism dish with vernonia leaves and peanut paste

cachupa-rica-cv.official_source_status: No Cabo Verde government recipe page was found in this pass. Cape Verde travel/culture references consistently identify cachupa as the national dish.
cachupa-rica-cv.review_summary: The Cachupa Rica page is sound: hominy corn, beans, vegetables, meats or sausage, and slow cooking. The method used garlic, bay leaves, salt, and water that were not visible in ingredients.
recommended_changes: Keep hominy corn, beans, meats, sausage, cabbage, sweet potato, and onions.
Add garlic, bay leaves, salt, and water because the method uses them and sources list them.
Keep leftover fried cachupa with egg as a serving note rather than the main service.

sources.title: CapeVerde.com - Gastronomy
supports: cachupa as national dish, maize, onions, green bananas, manioc, sweet potatoes, squash, yams, tomatoes, cabbage, bacon, bay leaves, garlic, pimento, cachupa rica with meats or fish

sources.title: Cachupa - Wikipedia
supports: slow-cooked Cape Verde stew of hominy, beans, fish or meat, sausage, pork, beef, goat, or chicken, national dish, cachupa rica has more ingredients

sources.title: Marga's International Recipes - Cachupa Rica
supports: kidney beans, onions, bay leaves, garlic, oil, salt, beef, bacon, linguica, paprika, potatoes, cassava, slow cooking

canja-cv.official_source_status: No Cabo Verde government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Cape Verde culture references and broader Portuguese/Cape Verdean canja sources.
canja-cv.review_summary: The Canja de Galinha page is directionally sound as a Cape Verdean chicken and rice soup used for comfort, sickness, funerals, and family occasions. It needed water, salt, pepper, and optional herbs visible in ingredients.
recommended_changes: Keep chicken, rice, onion, garlic, carrots, lime, and comfort-food framing.
Add water, salt, black pepper, and parsley or green onion.
Do not over-spice it; canja is supposed to be simple and soothing.

fields.instruction: Add shredded chicken back to the soup. Season with salt, black pepper, parsley or green onion, and a generous squeeze of lime.

sources.title: CapeVerde.com - Gastronomy
supports: canja de galinha as a Cape Verde rice soup with chicken and a must at weddings

sources.title: Canja de galinha - Wikipedia
supports: Cape Verdeans eat canja for colds, funerals, New Year, birthdays, and family events; chicken, rice, vegetables, garlic, salt, pepper, herbs

sources.title: Souptour - Canja from Cape Verde
supports: Cape Verdean chicken and rice soup comfort framing

pastel-diablo-cv.official_source_status: No Cabo Verde government recipe page was found in this pass. Portuguese-language recipe archives and Cape Verdean food writers agree on the tuna, chile, sweet-potato, and cornmeal pattern.
pastel-diablo-cv.review_summary: The previous Pastel com Diabo Dentro page had the right tuna/chile idea but the dough was too generic. Stronger Cape Verdean sources use sweet potato plus cornmeal for the dough, with a spicy tuna filling seasoned with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, vinegar or lemon, and salt.
recommended_changes: Replace all-purpose flour dough with sweet potato and cornmeal dough.
Add garlic, vinegar or lime, and salt to the tuna filling.
Keep tuna and malagueta as the default filling; beef/pork versions exist but are less aligned with the stronger Cape Verdean sources found here.

recipe.description: Cape Verdean fried sweet-potato and cornmeal pastries stuffed with a fiery tuna, tomato, onion, garlic, and malagueta filling.
recipe.story: These fiery little pastries are a staple of Cape Verdean snacks and celebrations. The devil inside is the sharp heat of malagueta and the savory tuna filling tucked into a sweet-potato and cornmeal dough before frying.

fields.title: Make the dough
fields.instruction: Boil sweet potatoes until tender, then peel and mash smooth. Mix in cornmeal gradually, adding a little sweet potato cooking water as needed, until the dough is moldable and does not crack. Rest covered for 30 minutes.
fields.tip: The dough should be soft enough to shape but firm enough to hold the filling.

fields.instruction: Fry onion until soft. Add garlic, tomatoes, and malagueta peppers, then cook 5 minutes. Add tuna, vinegar or lime, and salt, and cook 5 more minutes until dry. Season well.
fields.tip: The filling must be dry because wet filling makes soggy pastels.

sources.title: Rotas Gastronomicas Cabo Verde - Pastel com diabo dentro
supports: sweet potato and cornmeal dough, tuna, tomato, onion, garlic, malagueta, olive oil, vinegar or lemon, frying

sources.title: Receitas e Menus - Pasteis com Diabo Dentro
supports: sweet potato and cornmeal dough, tuna in oil, palm oil, onion, garlic, malagueta, tomato pulp, salt, vinegar

sources.title: 196 flavors - Cape Verde Pastels
supports: Cape Verdean pastels often prepared with tuna or white fish

gozo-cf.official_source_status: No Central African Republic government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence comes from country food references and encyclopedic cuisine summaries.
gozo-cf.review_summary: The Gozo page is sound: cassava flour and water cooked into a stiff paste used with sauces and stews. No static recipe changes were needed.
recommended_changes: Keep cassava flour, water, optional salt, vigorous stirring, and sauce pairing.
Keep peanut, okra, meat, fish, or vegetable sauces as pairings.
Do not over-specify one sauce as mandatory.

sources.title: CountryReports - Central African Republic recipes and diet
supports: cassava staple, goza paste made from cassava flour and water, served with meat, fish, or vegetable sauce

sources.title: Cuisine of the Central African Republic - Wikipedia
supports: cassava and manioc flour used for fufu in Central African Republic cuisine

kanda-ti-nyma-cf.official_source_status: No Central African Republic government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines culinary references for kanda and kanda ti nyma variants.
kanda-ti-nyma-cf.review_summary: The previous Kanda ti Nyma page had a peanut sauce, but missed the okra that distinguishes the kanda ti nyma pattern in multiple sources. It also used ground peanuts inside the meatballs, while sources more often bind the meatballs with egg and season them with garlic and chile, reserving peanuts for the sauce.
recommended_changes: Move away from peanut-bound meatballs and use egg, garlic, and chile in the meatball mix.
Add okra to the sauce as a defining ingredient.
Keep peanut butter sauce and gozo/rice/plantain serving options.

recipe.story: Kanda ti nyma means meat balls in Sango. In this researched version, the beef meatballs are seasoned with onion, garlic, chile, salt, and egg, then simmered in a peanut and okra sauce until tender. Serve it with gozo, rice, or plantain for a hearty Central African Republic table.

fields.instruction: Combine beef mince, beaten eggs, finely diced onion, garlic, chile, and salt in a bowl. Mix well with your hands until the mixture holds together. Shape into golf ball-sized meatballs.
fields.tip: The eggs help the meatballs hold while they simmer in the peanut-okra sauce.

fields.instruction: In the same pan, saute the diced onion until soft. Add okra and cook 5 minutes. Stir in the peanut butter and 1 cup of warm water, mixing until smooth.

sources.title: Marty Made It - Kanda ti Nyma
supports: ground beef, onions, eggs, chile, garlic, palm or groundnut oil, okra, peanut butter, warm water, salt, hot pepper powder

sources.title: Eat This NY - Kanda ti Nyma
supports: meatballs in peanut sauce thickened with okra, chili, onions, garlic, eggs, peanut butter, palm oil

sources.title: 196 flavors - Kanda
supports: Central African kanda variants, kanda ti nyma with okra and peanuts, traditional kanda with pumpkin seeds

sauce-graine-cf.official_source_status: No Central African Republic government recipe page was found in this pass, and direct CAR-specific sauce graine recipe evidence was weaker than for other dishes. Evidence supports palm fruit sauce broadly across West and Central Africa, but the CAR-specific framing should stay conservative.
sauce-graine-cf.review_summary: The Sauce Graine page is directionally sound as a palm-fruit sauce served with starch, fish, meat, and aromatics, but source coverage was not strongly CAR-specific. The practical gaps were palm oil for the saut? step and salt for seasoning.
recommended_changes: Keep palm fruit pulp, smoked fish, tomato, onion, chile, water, and gozo/rice pairing.
Add palm oil and salt because the method uses them.
Keep confidence low until a stronger CAR-specific source is located.

sources.title: Sauce graine - French Wikipedia
supports: red sauce made from oil-palm fruit pulp, widespread across sub-Saharan Africa with recipes varying by country

sources.title: Palm nut soup - Wikipedia
supports: palm fruit or palm cream base with smoked dried fish, meats, aromatics, eaten with fufu or rice, significant in West and Central African cuisines

sources.title: SmartChop - Palm Nut Sauce
supports: palm fruit extract, smoked protein, onion, tomato, water, salt, and rice/plantain service in a sauce graine pattern

moi-moi-ng.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references and encyclopedia support.
moi-moi-ng.review_summary: The AfroKitchen version follows the common Nigerian pattern: peeled black-eyed or brown beans, pepper, onion, oil, seasoning, optional crayfish or egg, and steaming in leaves, tins, ramekins, or foil until set.
recommended_changes: Keep boiled egg and crayfish optional because sources treat them as common add-ins, not mandatory.
Do not over-thicken the batter. Multiple references rely on enough water to steam into a soft pudding.

sources.title: All Nigerian Recipes - Tastiest Nigerian Moi Moi Recipe
All Nigerian Recipes - Prepare Nigerian Moi Moi with 5 Ingredients
Yummy Medley - Moi Moi Nigerian Bean Pudding

efo-riro-ng.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. Sources include regional food references, encyclopedia context, and diaspora reporting.
efo-riro-ng.review_summary: The common references agree on leafy greens, pepper blend, palm oil, locust beans or iru, crayfish, onion, and assorted meat or fish. Spinach is a practical diaspora substitute for Nigerian greens.
recommended_changes: Keep iru and crayfish visible because they define the classic Yoruba flavor.
Use spinach as an available substitute but name amaranth or efo shoko as the traditional green.

sources.title: Epicurious - Nigerian recipes, Efo Riro with Kemi Seriki
supports: dried shrimp or crayfish flavor profile

pepper-soup-ng.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. The reference set is regional food writing and encyclopedia context.
pepper-soup-ng.review_summary: The recipe follows the common Nigerian pepper soup format: meat or fish simmered with onion, pepper, ehu or pepper soup spice, scent leaf or uziza, and enough broth to serve hot.
recommended_changes: Keep protein flexible because sources show goat, chicken, catfish, cow foot, and assorted meat variants.
Name ehu or calabash nutmeg because several Nigerian sources identify it as a defining spice.

sources.title: All Nigerian Recipes - Nigerian Pepper Soup
All Nigerian Recipes - Nigerian Goat Meat Pepper Soup

puff-puff-ng.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. The references are culinary sources and encyclopedia context.
puff-puff-ng.review_summary: The recipe follows the widely repeated puff puff profile: flour, yeast, sugar, salt, warm water, optional nutmeg, one rise, then deep frying until golden.
recommended_changes: Keep nutmeg optional because it is common but not universal.
Use a 60 minute rise as a practical midpoint across sources.

sources.title: K's Cuisine - Nigerian Puff Puff Recipe
The Guardian - Puff puff Nigerian doughnuts recipe

waakye-gh.official_source_status: A Ghana Revenue Authority newsletter result mentions waakye, but this batch did not find an official recipe standard. The dish is verified through food references and sorghum industry documentation.
waakye-gh.review_summary: The core profile is stable across sources: rice and beans cooked together with sorghum or millet leaves for color, then served with shito, stew, eggs, plantain, fish, meat, salad, or spaghetti.
recommended_changes: Keep sorghum or millet leaves as the preferred ingredient and baking soda as fallback.
Describe accompaniments as optional because the rice-and-beans base is the recipe.

red-red-gh.official_source_status: No official Ghanaian recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The dish is confirmed by culinary references and encyclopedia context.
red-red-gh.review_summary: Sources consistently frame red red as a Ghanaian black-eyed pea stew cooked with palm oil or vegetable oil and served with fried plantain.
recommended_changes: Keep palm oil because it explains the dish's name and color.
Treat fish powder or smoked fish as optional, since vegetarian red red is also common.

sources.title: McCormick for Chefs - Red Red with Fried Plantains
Forward Food - Ghanaian Red Red Stew

banku-tilapia-gh.official_source_status: No official Ghanaian recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The recipe is supported by Ghanaian and regional culinary sources.
banku-tilapia-gh.review_summary: The recipe follows the common Ghanaian plate: banku made from fermented corn and cassava dough, served with grilled or fried tilapia and pepper sauce.
recommended_changes: Keep the banku itself simple: corn dough, cassava dough, water, and salt.
Treat the tilapia spice mix as flexible because source recipes vary by cook.

sources.title: Wikipedia - Banku
supports: served with fish and pepper sauce

sources.title: Guardian Nigeria - Ghana Traditional Dish, Banku And Tilapia
supports: banku and tilapia as Ghanaian dish

sources.title: Burnaby Neighbourhood House - Banku and Fried Tilapia PDF

githeri-ke.official_source_status: Kenya's Recipe Book of Common Mixed Dishes includes githeri. Additional accessible references confirm the maize-and-beans core.
githeri-ke.review_summary: The core dish is maize and beans boiled together. A light onion-tomato finish is a common modern approach, while the simple boiled version remains valid.
recommended_changes: Keep maize and beans as the non-negotiable core.
Mark tomatoes, carrots, and spices as a modern finish rather than the base identity.

mukimo-ke.official_source_status: Kenya's Recipe Book of Common Mixed Dishes includes mukimo. Additional Kenyan and humanitarian sources support the same ingredient pattern.
mukimo-ke.review_summary: The common pattern is potatoes mashed with maize, peas or beans, and greens. Onion or butter is optional, while pumpkin leaves or spinach provide the green color.
recommended_changes: Keep pumpkin leaves as traditional and spinach as an accessible substitute.
Do not over-blend. Mukimo can be smooth but should still feel like a mash.

sources.title: Go Visit Kenya - Kenya Mukimo Recipe
World Vision - In the kitchen, Mukimo recipe

malva-pudding-za.official_source_status: No official South African government recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The recipe is supported by encyclopedia, food media, and hospitality recipe references.
malva-pudding-za.review_summary: Sources consistently identify the South African dessert as an apricot-jam sponge leavened with bicarbonate and vinegar, then soaked with warm butter, sugar, and cream or milk sauce.
recommended_changes: Keep apricot jam, vinegar, and bicarbonate because they show up as defining ingredients.
Pour the sauce over the pudding while hot so it absorbs properly.

sources.title: Salty Ginger - Traditional South African Malva Pudding
